title: Shadow box scrapbook page
subtitle: Building a dimensional scrapbook layout with raised layers and recessed frames
excerpt: A shadow box layout raises selected elements off the page background using foam adhesive, creating depth and cast shadows that make a two-dimensional page read as a three-dimensional display. The technique requires planning the layer order before assembly: background first, mid-layer elements on foam dots, and the topmost focal element on the highest foam stack.
sourceNotes: Dimensional scrapbook layouts using foam adhesive are a contemporary paper-craft technique developed from the 1990s onward. No pre-1928 source applies. Synthesised from contemporary scrapbooking craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stack of one or more foam adhesive dots used to raise a scrapbook element above the page surface, creating visible depth and a cast shadow on the layer below.
The sequence in which elements are assembled on a scrapbook page, from the flat background up through the highest raised element. Planned before any adhesive is applied.

content.text: The cast shadow a raised element throws onto the layer below it is what creates the sense of depth. A
of 3 mm elevates an element enough to read clearly in photographs; 6 mm gives a more dramatic shadow for large focal elements.
before cutting anything. Lay all the elements on the background flat, decide which elements get foam lifts and how high, then start cutting and assembling.
Base page: 30 × 30 cm or 12 × 12 inch card stock. Patterned paper, plain card, or a distress-ink background. The base sits flat; nothing goes under it.
Adhere the base layer flat to the page card. This is the background: the lowest layer, no foam.
Cut the mid-layer elements (patterned paper mats, journalling boxes). Apply foam adhesive dots to the back at the corners and centre. Press into position.
Layer two or three foam dots directly on top of each other to increase the lift height for the focal element.
Apply photographs using photo corners (removable) or foam dots (permanent). The main photograph is typically the highest raised element.
Add flat embellishments (stickers, die-cuts) last. Flat elements on top of raised layers will bridge over the gap and bow; apply them to flat surfaces only.

attrs.symptom: Raised elements tilt or sag at the corners after assembly
attrs.cause: Too few foam dots supporting the element; the weight of the element overcomes the adhesive at the edges.

content.text: Use one foam dot per corner and one in the centre for any element larger than 50 × 50 mm. For heavy chipboard or thick card, use foam tape around the full perimeter rather than individual dots.
